behaviors.description: Clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) in contracts
behaviors.justification: The core failure risk is whether the real LLM can extract all required term types correctly. This is not a deterministic parsing task—it depends entirely on what the LLM produces. Mocking the LLM would hide extraction failures: a mock could return {parties, dates} while the real LLM misses obligations. Integration-level testing with mock LLM responses would always pass even if the real LLM fails systematically on penalty clauses. Only calling the real clause extractor with real LLMs exposes whether it actually identifies all term types.
behaviors.test_description: Create test contracts containing various key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) with some terms in complex phrasing. Call the real clause extractor on each. Verify that all term types are identified and correctly classified. Document any term types consistently missed to confirm the real LLM capability.

behaviors.description: Risk assessor flags problematic language including indirect or euphemistic phrasing
behaviors.justification: Detecting euphemistic language requires semantic understanding—'flexibility' for early termination, 'optimization' for price increases. This is fundamentally an LLM semantic task, not a pattern-matching one. Integration testing with mock LLM responses would always pass because the mock would be programmed to flag the phrases we tell it to. The real failure—the LLM missing or misinterpreting indirect language—only surfaces with real LLM calls. Rule-based systems would fail this behavior entirely; only a real LLM can handle the semantic subtlety.
behaviors.test_description: Create contracts with indirect risk language using euphemisms and contextual phrasing. Include both obvious risks and subtle ones (e.g., 'either party may suggest modifications' in a termination clause, 'reasonable cost optimization'). Call the real risk assessor. Verify it flags all problematic phrases and understand why—semantic understanding by the real LLM is being tested.

behaviors.description: Compliance checker correctly determines whether contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Two failure risks exist here: (1) the LLM misunderstands regulations or misses compliance issues (Agentic failure), and (2) the jurisdiction config isn't applied correctly (Integration concern). The primary risk is regulatory understanding—a Unit test of jurisdiction routing wouldn't catch the LLM producing incorrect compliance assessments. Integration testing with mock LLM responses would hide real failures where the LLM misinterprets EU GDPR requirements or misses CCPA retention rules. Only real LLM calls expose whether the checker actually understands the regulations for each jurisdiction.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real compliance checker with contracts for 3+ different jurisdictions (e.g., US, EU, Singapore), where each has different regulatory requirements. Verify correct compliance assessment for each jurisdiction. Include contracts with subtle compliance gaps to expose whether the real LLM correctly understands the rules. Confirm jurisdiction configuration is applied and LLM regulatory understanding is sound.

behaviors.description: Merge step preserves both conflicting assessments with attribution when two reviewers disagree about the same clause
behaviors.justification: The merge failure is deterministic: either it preserves both assessments and attribution, or it loses one or misattributes. This can be tested in isolation by providing pre-made mock outputs from the three reviewers and verifying the merge function logic. The function either handles conflicts correctly (Unit test pass) or loses/overwrites them (Unit test fail). There is a secondary risk about collecting outputs asynchronously (one reviewer times out, results lost before merge)—that would require Integration—but the stated behavior focuses on the merge step's preservation logic, not collection. A Unit test directly tests the merge function logic without depending on LLM output content.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock outputs from two reviewers that flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., Extractor: 'standard obligation', Risk Assessor: 'high-risk language'). Pass these to the merge function. Verify both assessments appear in the merged output with clear attribution (e.g., 'Extractor says X, Risk Assessor says Y'). Also test: merge correctly attributes findings and doesn't lose or overwrite conflicting claims.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline completes correctly for a real contract, dispatching to all three reviewers in parallel, collecting results, and merging without losing any findings
behaviors.justification: This is explicitly an end-to-end test: 'real contract', 'without losing any findings'. The real failures are orchestration-level: one reviewer times out and its results don't reach merge, outputs are dropped during async collection, or the pipeline doesn't handle partial/late results. Integration testing with mock LLMs wouldn't expose this—you'd need the real parallel execution and failure modes. Agentic testing of individual reviewers wouldn't catch orchestration failures like 'compliance checker result is lost when risk assessor finishes late'. This requires multiple agentic steps (three parallel LLM calls) orchestrated and merged together—the full agent pipeline. Only Workflow-level testing of the complete dispatch→collect→merge sequence exposes whether all findings are preserved.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline with a real contract (50+ clauses recommended for realistic scope). Dispatch to all three reviewers in parallel using real LLM calls. Collect results from all three (with realistic timeouts/delays if possible). Merge outputs into a unified report. Verify: (1) the pipeline completes successfully, (2) findings from all three reviewers appear in the final report, (3) no duplicate or conflicting findings are lost, (4) attribution is preserved for each reviewer. Document any lost findings or attribution errors.
